body:
CS - 5/13/2026
Coach - mala

9am - woke up. Had water with lemon.

10–11am we did a workout on YouTube. Reported she feels like she may be
ready to return to yoga classes within the next couple of days.

Went over schedule for today. I asked if her adviser had replied to her
email yet and she said no.I showed her a computer shortcut I discovered by
accident on my keyboard. She became interested in it and then started
looking up more shortcuts on Youtube.  She wrote down the different
commands and shortcuts for both of us to refer back to.

1:30pm meds.

2:30–4pm met with her mum to look at furniture and design ideas for the
house. Mood  overall were good.

Although, while C was at the counter paying for something.  her mum shared
privately with me that a friend had mistaken their dinner plans for next
week when they were actually scheduled for tomorrow and she was going to be
annoyed if her friend cancels.  Her mum commented that she finds C
exhausting and does not always like being around her too much, especially
during dinners with her dad. She then asked if I also find C exhausting. I
responded that C has been doing really well and is really trying to find
friends to do things with.  Mum wished C could find a friend who had
similar mental illness she could bond with.

5–7pm had a light dinner

7:15pm Yin yoga class. The class was more meditation-focused and calming
than physically demanding.

9pm meds.

Throughout the day she was analyzing and joking about her diagnosis. She
broke down the word “schizoaffective” and joked that it means she is
“effective.” I asked why she was reframing it that way and she said she
does not want the diagnosis to hold power over her. She is still trying to
challenge her illness and saying it was all circumstantial by outside
events.  Gave her space to process this. I then asked if she had been
seeing her therapist regularly and suggested it could also be something
useful to continue exploring in therapy. She stated that she likes her
therapist.

Today she also showed me a chronological list she has been putting together
of her treatments, hospitalizations, and past medications. She said she is
trying to better understand the timeline of her mental health history and
treatment progression.
( Seems like with a lot of downtime she's focusing on this )

Continued issues with attention. Cannot multitask. She forgot the turkey
bacon on the stove after walking away to get ready. I was in the kitchen so
I was checking on it, but when she returned I asked if she had forgotten
anything and she initially said no. After I reminded her about the stove,
she shrieked and said she needs to put a bigger sign above the oven to
remind herself. She also continues to leave drawers, cupboards, and doors
open after getting items out.

Decided she would rather do a life -drawing class than go to the concert.
So she signed us both to do the drawing class on Sunday instead.
 
 ----
Secured by Paubox - HITRUST certified
https://www.paubox.com
----

namiwalks - DonorDrive®                              Hi Doro, 

 By registering for NAMIWalks Westchester, you moved NAMI one step closer
to Mental Health for All. 

 When it comes to mental health, together, we are stronger. Together, we
break down barriers. Together, we create hope. *Together, we are
NAMIWalks.*

 Your commitment:

   - Promotes awareness of mental health and reduces stigma.
  - Raises funds for NAMI’s free, top-rated mental health programs.
  - Builds community to let people know they are not alone. 
 
 Here are *six easy ways *to show your community that you are NAMIWalks: 

   - Make a self-donation. Show NAMIWalks matters to you by making a
donation to your efforts - any amount makes a difference! 
  - *Get personal. *You’ve joined a national movement to reduce stigma by
sharing our stories - our why - with others. Update your participant page
<https://mandrillapp.com/track/click/30041712/namiwalks?> to include your photo
and your story <https://mandrillapp.com/track/click/30041712/namiwalks?>...share your
why! 
  - *Engage your network. *Family, friends, and coworkers are your network
of influence <https://mandrillapp.com/track/click/30041712/www?>. Reach
out today to ask for their support by making a donation or joining you on
Walk Day.  
  - *Find your best tools. *Emails, letters, social media, and more…find
the best tools to communicate with your network. We highly recommend
sending emails through your participant dashboard
<https://mandrillapp.com/track/click/30041712/namiwalks?>! 
  - *Share on social media. *Showcase to the world that you are NAMIWalks
with this photo frame <https://mandrillapp.com/track/click/30041712/namiwalks?> or use our

share on social media with a caption promoting NAMIWalks! Need help with a
caption? Check out some samples here
<https://mandrillapp.com/track/click/30041712/namiwalks?>. 
  - *Download the Fundraise4NAMI App. *Fundraise on the go by visiting the
App Store
<https://mandrillapp.com/track/click/30041712/apps?> or
Google Play Store
<https://mandrillapp.com/track/click/30041712/play?>
to download the app now! You’ll be able to text or email a donation link
to your contacts, share your QR code with prospective donors, post updates
on social media, get notifications when you receive a donation…and
more! 
 
 You are now part of a nationwide community of wellness, joy, and hope. We
cannot wait to see you at NAMIWalks Westchester proudly wearing your 2026
T-shirt <https://mandrillapp.com/track/click/30041712/namiwalks?> - earned when you raise $100
or more by Walk Day!  

 View our Participant Guidebook <https://mandrillapp.com/track/click/30041712/namiwalks?>
and Participant Resource Center <https://mandrillapp.com/track/click/30041712/namiwalks?>
for additional tips, tricks, and tools to amplify your fundraising and
awareness-building efforts for NAMIWalks.  

 Together, we are strong. Together, we are hopeful. *Together We Are
NAMIWalks.*

body: _Schema doc sync — 2026-05-13_  Reviewed commits in `doromind/doro` since the last `docs(data)` sync (PR #4335, `3504f227c`).  _Schema deltas:_ none — `git log 3504f227c..HEAD -- core/data/prisma/schema.prisma core/data/prisma/migrations` is empty.  _Behavior change worth a doc:_ PR #4374 (`feat: set needs attention when prospects enter activation`) introduced `NEEDS_ATTENTION_ON_ENTERING_STAGES` in `core/data/src/prospect-stage-rules.ts`. `transitionProspectStage` now auto-sets `ProspectFields.needsAttention = true` whenever a prospect _enters_ a stage in that set (currently only `ACTIVATION`) from a different stage — including initial `ProspectFields` creation. The schema's `///` comments and `docs/prospect-stage-transitions.md` didn't capture this.  _Proposed (PR opened):_ • `core/data/prisma/schema.prisma` — add a `///` doc on `ProspectFields.needsAttention` describing the auto-flag and pointing at the rule constant + transitions doc. • `docs/prospect-stage-transitions.md` — append one sentence to the "Side Effects on Stage Change" section. • `core/data/prisma/schema_docs.md` — _not_ updated; this auto-flag is a behavior detail, not an NL→SQL routing signal, and the doc is meant to stay focused.  PR: <https://github.com/doromind/doro/pull/4409>

body:
*Today's Care Team Roundtable*
<!subteam^S08SCMXLWQH> <!subteam^S08QRJ3TX42> Team, know we said that we'd keep standing meetings in place this month, but we have a number of conflicts today so I'm going to go ahead and pull down our time at 4:30EST.  *I am working with Keren* *Minna* *Hannah Bock to find a new block that will more consistently work for this team to convene* as I want to make sure we have a standing time that we can come together and collaborate!

As I come up to speed and as we get to know each other, I'm also going to *set up a standing "office hours" series starting next week*. These are _completely optional,_ but I'll plan to earmark these times for you all as best as possible.  Feel free to drop in whenever you are able. My goal is to provide an informal, unstructured space where we can connect in real time. We can use this time to troubleshoot pending items, address any particular questions or concerns, or simply take a moment to catch up and step away from the daily routine.

Talk soon - and as always, holler if you need anything!

body:
Kate DouglasJon I’ve been digging into message tagging/flagging and I think there’s a relatively low-lift change we could make that would materially improve routing and visibility into team workload.

Right now our categories are too broad (e.g. “medications”, “symptoms”), which means clinically different work gets grouped together — for example, medication refills vs medication changes, or symptoms vs new labs to review. That makes it harder to route correctly and also limits how well we can understand where time is actually going.

_This is how we categorize messages today:_
• `symptoms_and_side_effects` 
• `medications`
• `scheduling`
• `billing_and_insurance`
• `tech_support`
• `care_coordination`
• `administrative`
• `other`

My thinking is we introduce a slightly more granular, workflow-based set of categories (no changes to the chat experience required), and use those to:
1. Better route messages to the right role (e.g. NP vs Guide vs Coach)
2. Quantify volume by type of work (not just topic)
3. Create a foundation for future AI automations (e.g. refill handling vs clinical review)
Would love your input, especially on where more distinction would be most operationally useful. If we can agree on this set of tags, and the primary owner of each workflow I think this could be something we can test with the current iteration of chat.

<https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/16IkvPybGJuxn4nW2vhVJ9CKGLcn7NxI6/edit?gid=142795804#gid=142795804>

body:
TUESDAY, MAY 12, 2026
*Ken's Daily Briefing*
AI & Schizophrenia Research Digest
Today's news surfaces a structural betrayal and a structural breakthrough in the same cycle. Nursing homes are gaming federal quality metrics by misdiagnosing residents with schizophrenia -- using the diagnosis as cover for inappropriate antipsychotic prescribing. Simultaneously, immunopsychiatry research confirms that 1-5% of the 23 million people diagnosed with schizophrenia may have treatable autoimmune conditions. On the AI front, hackers used a model to find a zero-day vulnerability for 2FA bypass, while OpenAI expanded GPT-5.5-Cyber access to verified defenders the same week. For Doro Mind, the parallel is direct: bad diagnostics protect institutions, not patients.
*ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE*
*<https://www.nytimes.com/2026/05/08/magazine/ai-populism-backlash-altman.html|A.I. Populism Is Here. And No One Is Ready.>*
New York Times
Silicon Valley spent years worrying about existential AI risk to the world while ignoring the people it was disrupting. David Wallace-Wells argues the backlash is now arriving -- and the oligarchs who built these systems have no plan for it.
*<https://www.cnbc.com/2026/05/11/google-thwarts-effort-hacker-group-use-ai-mass-exploitation-event.html|Google Thwarts AI-Assisted Zero-Day Exploit>*
CNBC
A hacker group used an AI model to find a zero-day vulnerability enabling two-factor authentication bypass. Google says its counter-discovery likely prevented what would have been a mass exploitation event.
*<https://openai.com/index/gpt-5-5-with-trusted-access-for-cyber|OpenAI Launches GPT-5.5-Cyber for Defenders>*
OpenAI
OpenAI expands its Trusted Access program to verified cybersecurity researchers, deploying GPT-5.5-Cyber specifically to accelerate vulnerability research and protect critical infrastructure -- same week hackers weaponized a comparable model class.
*<https://www.nytimes.com/2026/05/07/business/spacex-chips-terafab.html|SpaceX Plans $55 Billion AI Chip Factory>*
New York Times
Musk's Terafab semiconductor factory is the latest bet that AI dominance flows from compute ownership. At $55B it would be one of the largest chip manufacturing investments in U.S. history.
*<https://www.wired.com/story/the-new-wild-west-of-ai-kids-toys/|The New Wild West of AI Kids' Toys>*
Wired
Connected AI companions for children are proliferating with no regulatory framework in place. They are reshaping make-believe and bedtime routines, and some lawmakers are moving to ban them outright.
*SCHIZOPHRENIA RESEARCH*
*<https://www.tpr.org/podcast/petrie-dish/2026-05-05/when-schizophrenia-is-a-treatable-autoimmune-disease|When Schizophrenia Is a Treatable Autoimmune Disease>*
Texas Public Radio
Dr. Anthony Zoghbi at Baylor identified cases -- including April Burrell, institutionalized 26 years -- where the diagnosis was actually autoimmune encephalitis. Between 1-5% of the 23 million global schizophrenia patients may have a treatable autoimmune condition. That is over one million people.
*<https://oig.hhs.gov/reports/all/2026/nursing-homes-inappropriately-diagnosed-residents-with-schizophrenia-to-mask-the-misuse-of-antipsychotic-drugs/|Nursing Homes Misdiagnosed Residents to Hide Antipsychotic Misuse>*
HHS Office of Inspector General
An OIG investigation of 40 nursing facilities found a systematic pattern: false schizophrenia diagnoses issued to evade CMS quality metrics and justify inappropriate antipsychotic prescriptions in dementia patients. All three remediation recommendations remain unimplemented -- updates expected September 2026.
*<https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-026-50969-2|Disrupted Neural Timing in Schizophrenia Spectrum Disorder>*
Nature / Scientific Reports
An fMRI study published this week finds that neural response timing during hand movement preparation is measurably disrupted in schizophrenia spectrum disorder, adding to the growing body of biomarker research that could improve diagnostic precision.
*THE SO-WHAT?*
The OIG nursing home report and the autoimmune schizophrenia research are two faces of the same failure. The schizophrenia diagnosis is being used as a shield -- protecting institutions from oversight -- and as a catch-all that traps patients who have something else entirely. Doro Mind's premise is that better triage and treatment matching reduces the human cost of this diagnostic incoherence. The AI story of the week is a useful analogy: attackers and defenders used the same model class in the same week. The same pattern-recognition capability that lets a bad actor find a zero-day can find anomalous diagnosis clusters in nursing home billing data. The tool is neutral. Who deploys it first is not.
